text: The surgeon uses a Livac device, consisting of a soft silicon ring with suction tubing, to stabilize the spleen during laparoscopic splenectomy. The device creates a vacuum between two planar surfaces to facilitate splenic retraction.

The surgeon performs a splenectomy using two ports and a Teleflex mini-lap clutch in the right semilateral decubitus position. The moderately large spleen falls onto the hilum, obscuring it. A small Livac retractor is inserted at the umbilical port site, positioned between the spleen and the inner chest wall, and suction is activated to create a vacuum. The lower pole attachments are cleared using a 5mm ligature sealer divider, and dissection proceeds to the hilum where the lower pole vessels are individually dissected and divided with ligature between Weck clips. A color change in the spleen is visible.

The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly to clear medial attachments and expose vessels supplying the mid and upper pole of the spleen. The pancreas is visible extending close to the spleen but remains clear of the dissection area. The artery is clipped before the vein, and both are divided using a ligature device.

The spleen is fully devascularized and mobilized from a medial approach, with the Livac retractor holding it in place. The retractor is not visible as it is positioned on the opposite side of the spleen, allowing the surgical assistant to focus on guiding the laparoscope. The spleen remains stable due to the suction effect of the Livac retractor.

The Livac retractor is released, and the suction is visibly removed from the spleen, showing embossing on its surface without any bleeding. The spleen is maneuvered into a 15mm EndoCatch bag, where it is morselated using sponge-holding forceps at the Hasson port site for specimen evacuation.
This is her abdomen three days after surgery.

The surgeon clears the lower pole attachments of the vascular spleen using a ligature device. Despite suboptimal exposure with three ports, a large Livac retractor is inserted, forming a visible suction seal. The hilar vessels are dissected individually, with arteries divided before veins using ligature between Weck clips.

The surgeon completes the hilar dissection of the large spleen using three laparoscopic ports. The Livac retractor is released, and the initial embossing on the spleen flattens out within minutes. The patient has a modest incision in the left iliac fossa and two five-millimeter ports, recovering uneventfully.

Both cases took about two minutes from insertion to retraction, and this did not interfere with the field of dissection. No assistance retraction was required, early control of hilar vessels was attained, and retraction was only required for thirty to thirty-nine minutes in patients with enlarged spleens.
